summary:
Portuguese exploration of Africa (c. 1415–1498 AD) was the first systematic program of oceanic exploration in European history — a deliberate, state-directed enterprise undertaken over nearly a century that progressively extended European geographical knowledge of the African coastline from Morocco to East Africa and ultimately found the sea route to India, transforming the world's commercial geography and inaugurating the [[event:97|Age of Exploration]]. Initiated under [[event:3055|Prince Henry the Navigator's]] organizational direction from 1415, the program sent expeditions year after year down the African coast, each one advancing a little further than the last.

The exploration's technical innovations were crucial to its success: the development of the caravel — a light, maneuverable ship capable of sailing closer to the wind than earlier vessels and therefore able to return northward against the prevailing winds that had previously made southward exploration a one-way trip — and the systematic improvement of celestial navigation using the astrolabe and later the quadrant to determine latitude by solar and stellar observation. By 1434, Gil Eanes had rounded Cape Bojador (the psychological barrier beyond which European sailors had feared the sea was too hot or monsters lurked); by 1488, [[event:3056|Bartholomew Dias rounded the Cape of Good Hope]]; by 1498, [[event:2290|Vasco da Gama reached India]]. The African exploration also established Portuguese trading posts (feitorias) on the West African coast, initiating the Atlantic slave trade and the extraction of gold and ivory that would fund continued exploration and make Portugal briefly one of the wealthiest states in Europe.
